source_title: Optic nerve injury secondary to endoscopic sinus surgery: an analysis of three cases.
note_excerpt: The source dataset row describes a 53-year-old woman who developed immediate no-light-perception vision loss in the right eye after endoscopic sinus surgery under local anesthesia. Outside orbital CT showed a small right medial wall fracture and thick bony septum in the right ethmoid area. She received steroid pulse therapy followed by oral prednisolone, visual evoked potential testing showed absent right optic nerve activity, orbital MRI showed posterior optic nerve sheath enhancement with an abnormal focus near the medial rectus near the orbital apex, and vision remained absent at 2-month follow-up.
dataset_conversation_excerpt:
Doctor: Hello, I am Dr. Smith. How can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, I had endoscopic sinus surgery two days ago and now I cannot see with my right eye.
Doctor: Did the vision loss happen immediately after the surgery?
Patient: Yes. The next day I had no light perception in the right eye.
Doctor: What testing has been done so far?
Patient: Orbital CT showed a small right medial wall fracture, the visual evoked potential test showed no right optic nerve activity, and MRI showed enhancement around the posterior optic nerve sheath.
provenance_note: This benchmark case was rebuilt from the downloaded dataset row rather than invented from scratch. The benchmark transcript, chart note, and ground truth were lightly rewritten to remove article discussion text, eliminate obvious answer leakage, and keep only the patient-specific clinical facts needed for evaluation.
